I think this should be formalized as separate, not homogenized, and here's what I mean.

The service state values, which come from the status request to Vera, are the master set of device values and states. These are the Pascal-case names.

The shortcode values, which come from the sdata request to Vera, are a small subset of the service state that is defined by each device's service file (S_device.XML). 

I think get_complex_value() should be deprecated and a new method added named get_service_state() or get_service_value(), which is more in keeping with Vera's/UPnP's terminology and familiar to the Vera developer community, and get_value() should be deprecated and a new method added called get_shortcode_value() or similar. 

Vera clients should be well aware of the differences between these values. Combining them into a single get_value() call creates the opportunity for collisions, and more confusion than this odd disconnect by Vera already creates. 

It's worth nothing that the status request (full state variable results) can also accept the DataVersion parameter, to return incremental changes to prior requests, as lu_sdata does in get_changed_devices(). In my perfect world, pyvera would not have used the shortcode/sdata here, but rather status, thus making the full suite of changes a device could see available to subscribers. As it stands today, only the subset is published.

The whole problem arises because the DashCast app is odd.

It has two idiosyncrasies:

a) once loaded it, by design becomes unresponsive (because it redirects to the casted webpage which has no idea it's being rendered in Chromecast) (this has been solved previously by killing it and now by force loading the app every time)

b) when you load the chromecast app, it's initial state is `Application is Starting` https://github.com/stestagg/dashcast/blob/master/receiver.html#L163 

Only eventually the status becomes `Application Ready` https://github.com/stestagg/dashcast/blob/master/receiver.html#L143

The changes I did in `CastStatusListener` (which in the end of the day are not that ugly), explicitly set the state to ready only when it's really ready (by waiting for the `Application Ready` status).

And since I live in Brazil, which is the other side of earth, the internet has much more latency than in Europe and the US because we are very far and light is not that fast.

Australians have it worse. As soon as `catt` becomes a worldwide hit they will fill most of the bugs if we leave the race condition!

I am completely against leaving a race condition there. It will make casting a url something that will randomly fail, more often if there is more latency and after we forget all this, we will never ever fix it because, as with every race condition, even reproducing is a challenge. We know the problem, we have the solution ready and we are willing not to use it. Sorry to say that, but this is negligence. 

Thinking about it, the discussion here is the most technical solution versus the least intrusive solution. The former is always my choice.

`DashCast` needs special treatment and that's clearly defined in `CastStatusListener`. We can't hide away from that fact. `CastStatusListener` is the right place to deal with it.

Also, any coder in the future will see that. I can, of course, improve the comments, but even if we leave the code as is, I don't believe anybody would ever delete any of the code from `CastStatusListener` by accident in the future, for cleanup's sake. (different from a flag that arbitrarily and unintuitively set's the ready state to false)

Guys, sorry for not agreeing. This is just my opinion and I do respect if you decide otherwise. Please don't get offended by me not agreeing.

Originally this was `float(norm)`, and that is not appropriate. Suppose you have the distribution:

```
[0, 0, ... , 0, 1]
```

If the `num_quantized_bins` is `3`, then you theoretically should get:

```
[0, 0, ... , 1/3, 1/3, 1/3]
```

instead of:

```
[0, 0, ..., 1, 1, 1]
```

To make this more clear, suppose your original dist is:

```
[0, 0, 0, ... , 1/3, 1/3, 1/3]
```

This should be equivalent after quantization as the first distribution, but it isn't. Under the rules, you would get the same array back, where you have `[..., 1/3, 1/3 ,1/3]`, and the first distribution would give you `[..., 1, 1, 1]`, off by the multiplier.

Sorry to bring this up _after_ the PR has been merged, but I think this pattern is less than ideal: using this means that all the cases outside of a class now have a log source of "airflow.utils.log.LoggingMixin.LoggingMixin":

```
In [7]: from airflow.utils.log.LoggingMixin import LoggingMixin

In [8]: l = LoggingMixin().logger

In [9]: l.name
Out[9]: 'airflow.utils.log.LoggingMixin.LoggingMixin'
```

Compare this with:

```
In [10]: from airflow.contrib.hooks.bigquery_hook import BigQueryHook

In [11]: BigQueryHook().logger.name
Out[11]: 'airflow.contrib.hooks.bigquery_hook.BigQueryHook'
```

That advantage of the latter is that different classes can be set at different level log. In a case like this where we need to log from outside a class we loose that ability.

That said what we do is probably not worse in many cases where we were just using the root logger, this just happens to be one of the few cases that was using a differently named logger. So ideally in cases like this we'd somehow pass `__module__` in, rather than getting the module from the class.

An example of how this is different:

```
In [2]: from airflow.contrib.hooks.bigquery_hook import BigQueryHook

In [3]: from airflow.utils.log.LoggingMixin import LoggingMixin

In [4]: lm = LoggingMixin()

In [5]: h = BigQueryHook()

In [6]: h.logger.info("hook")
[2017-09-13 09:16:14,422] {<ipython-input-6-d9ef8875e3af>:1} INFO - hook

In [7]: lm.logger.info("mixin")
[2017-09-13 09:16:22,798] {<ipython-input-7-f4ed3f594263>:1} INFO - mixin

In [8]: import logging

In [9]: logging.getLogger('airflow.contrib').setLevel(logging.ERROR)

In [10]: lm.logger.info("mixin")
[2017-09-13 09:17:58,043] {<ipython-input-10-f4ed3f594263>:1} INFO - mixin

In [11]: h.logger.info("hook")

In [12]: # Note how there was no log produced

In [13]: h.logger.error("but error still is")
[2017-09-13 09:19:32,870] {<ipython-input-13-0b7830c22006>:1} ERROR - but error still is
```

```python
def a():

 105,
 6,
 106,
 7,
 107,
 8,
 108,

```

No, what I'm trying to avoid is magic methods like `__len__`, etc. on things that may not have any definition of that. A peer cannot be less than another peer. Maybe `__len__` on a `PeerHeap` is okay because you want to determine the number of objects inside the heap.

But I would rather not have the implementation of the heap algorithm itself depend on magic methods. `HeapOperation` is the algorithm's interface to the underlying storage. It should not rely on anything implemented on the `HeapOperation` besides what was specified in the interface above. ```python
if isinstance(exception, (Exception, BaseException):

```
if isinstance(exception, BaseException):
```

My reference is always the (recent) Python stdlib for style. It's not considered "pythonic" to have superfluous characters.  The typical example is for tuple.

```python
one = two, three, four     # do this
one = (two, three, four)     # not this
```
One exception of avoiding \ to break a line.

e.g. do:

```python
import (one, two,
            three)     # yes!!
import one, two, \
           three   # boooo!
```

So I guess my question is: what's the benefit for our code base to do this differently ?  If none, I am +1 to use Python stdlib conventions. (We should check of course that it's wlays done like that there :))
In python3 mock is, but the difference I do is more Python functionality versus Project specific functionality

Yes.  If you subclass from RingBuilder (which I did), then `self.__class__` is the subtype, not RingBuidler, and `super(self.__class__, self)` is `RingBuilder`, not `StampBuilder`, which leads to an infinite loop.

I had seen the trick about writing `super(self.__class__, self)` on StackOverflow somewhere, and I thought it was a clever way to make sure you do the right thing.  But it turns out it breaks in this kind of use case, so I think writing out the actual class name is better practice in general.

I'm still *really* unhappy with tweaking the artificial numerical limits in the global config every time we run up against one of them:

- If the numbers are actually meaningful (almost never), then we should enforce them.
- If the numbers are "more guidelines, really", then disabling them locally allows them to be as useful as they were when we come across a solitary case where the guideline is deemed un-useful.
- If they are basically random opinions pulled from the air at Logilab, then we should disable them altogether.

In this case, I think the "too many attributes" bit is not a terrible guideline, but needs to be conditioned by the fact that we map APIs not under our control:  adoptiong the "don't share testing state between unrelated methods" strategy for the mock connections means the limit isn't helpful.

> ...our internal customer wants to import tensorflow in setup.py so that they can load all the tensorflow includes into include_dirs. Would there be a way around this, if we weren't going to include deps inpython_dist?

the dependencies declared on this target would never be made available to the executing `setup.py` for use in the current model. they would only be pulled in transitively for any target roots and resolved into deps for a final pex product (pex, chroot or otherwise).

setuptools/`setup.py` et al already make an affordance for this case with `setup_requires`. this ensures the declared `setup_requires` deps are present at *setup.py execution* time vs *install* time. making it so that the user could declare "i need tensorflow during setup.py execution time" would, afaict, involve plumbing full support for `setup_requires` in `SetupPyRunner` via either native `setup.py` declarations or BUILD file declarations. I think from the pants/pex side, this would involve composing a setup-time pex/chroot and executing the `setup.py` inside of that where it'd be able to `import tensorflow` from a hermetic environment.

we typically don't deal with `setup_requires` much in pants/pex land because of the emphasis on bdists (wherein `setup.py` is pre-executed for us) for repeatability - but it'd certainly be a nice feature to have for `python_dist` consumption, since it'd bring us closer to parity with `pip`.

I think this comment is an important implementation detail! But right now it's just worded weirdly. Basically, the behavior of `join` is:

```
In [2]: t = Table().with_columns('x', [1, 2, 3], 'y', [10, 11, 12])
In [13]: t2 = Table().with_columns('x', [1, 1, 3], 'z', [100, 105, 110])

In [12]: t
Out[12]:
x    | y
1    | 10
2    | 11
3    | 12
In [14]: t2 # t2 has x = 1 twice
Out[14]:
x    | z
1    | 100
1    | 105
3    | 110

# But when we join, only the first 'x' value in t2 is kept
In [15]: t.join('x', t2)
Out[15]:
x    | y    | z
1    | 10   | 100
3    | 12   | 110

# Same for the opposite join
In [17]: t2.join('x', t)
Out[17]:
x    | z    | y
1    | 100  | 10
1    | 105  | 10
3    | 110  | 12
```

This behavior is a bit strange but it tends to work out for students. I think we should clarify this behavior in the docs and probably add examples demonstrating this.

That's a nicely detailed and well formulated description. May I add a few comments and suggestions? (I am using **bold** font to indicate added and edited portions of the text.

**Web** browsers and other software commonly require a certificate when making a secure connection to an Internet service. ...

[Comment: There are also image browsers, file browsers, etc. that are working locally.]

The certificate contains a public key. The certificate's recipient—the operator of the site or service described by the certificate—also has the corresponding private key, which needs to be kept secret. The certificate is used to identify the server when establishing a secure connection **and to encrypt the communication for this connection**. 

> The main security benefit of using a certificate is the prevention of "man-in-the-middle" attacks, in which someone in between you and the server you think you are talking to is able to spy on you or insert their own (harmful) content.

That's not quite right. That's only one security benefit. (And I wouldn't call it a "benefit", rather it is the purpose.)
But since the encryption of the secure channel relies on the key (certificate), having a certificate/key is a necessity.  Hence, I suggest something like this:

The certificate contains a public key. The certificate's recipient—the operator of the site or service described by the certificate—also has the corresponding private key, which needs to be kept secret. **There are two roles that a certificate plays in a secure connection:  to identify the server when establishing the connection and to encrypt the communication for the connection**. The certificate includes information about the public key, information about the server identity, and the digital signature of the certificate authority that issued the certificate. If the issuer is trusted by the software that initiates the communication, and the signature is valid, then the certificate confirms that the public key can be used to communicate securely with the server identified by the certificate. **One of the purposes of identifying the server is to prevent** "man-in-the-middle" attacks, in which someone **in-between** you and the server you think you are talking to is able to spy on you or insert their own (harmful) content.

[Note the dash in *in-between*, between the words ;-)  Ref: https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/in-between ]

Would it make sense listing all components of the certificate explicitly and together -- somewhere around here?


> Certbot introduces the concept of a lineage, which is a collection of all the versions of a certificate and its associated files from renewal to renewal, plus Certbot configuration information for that certificate. 

What are the "associated files" that are neither parts of the certficate, not Certbot configuration information? Do you mean CSRs? Whatever you mean by them, why not list all components of a lineage explicitly?


> You can view a _list of lineages_, and their names, by running certbot _certificates_; for most operations with Certbot, you can _specify which lineage_ you want to act on using _--cert-name_.

I understand that you correctly described how Certbot works, but doesn't this sentence expose some awkwardness of the terminology used by Certbot? A naive question: if I want to view a list of *lineages* why don't I use a command `certbot lineages`? And if I want to specify which *lineage*, why don't I use --lineage-name? ;-)
I am not suggesting to introduce these commands; the existing commands are more intuitive. Rather, I am demonstrating why the notion of "lineage" is confusing. 

Actually, I'd suggest rephrasing this sentence to avoid this awkwardness as follows:

You can view a list of **certificate** lineages, and their names, by running `certbot certificates`. For most operations with Certbot, you can specify which **certificate** ~~lineage~~ you want to act on using `--cert-name`.

I think certbot is primarily acting on a certificate, not on a lineage. This is consistent with your description of the lineage and with certbot's commands:   you request, obtain, renew, modify, remove  a **certificate**, not a _lineage_. (You modify the lineage by modifying [replacing] the current certificate) You delete the _lineage_. (I've never done that yet, but I assume the entire lineage is deleted, right?)     And `certbot --help` output talks about acting on **certificates**, not on lineages.
